definition: 1. (Skt wing, side; half of anything; half of a lunar month). See bhramara-, kṛṣṇa-, śukla-. 2. (Skt) side, party, section, following, ``denomination'', sect; to be on the side of, follow the way of, in the way of, imitating, like. See also a-, bhikṣuka-, boddha-, buddha-, eka-, muni-, prati-, sarwa-, śaiwa-, siddha-, śiwa-, su-, tri-, wana-, weṣṇawa-, wi-, wibhinna-, wiṣṇu-, yogita-. 3. definition: ) attractive or captivating quality; appeal; profit, advantage, benefit, good, pleasure, rest? The best explanation of this problematic word seems to be: pālapan, the place where an ``alap'' is found, the quality which can ``aṅalap hati'', which has attraction, also: of usefulness by which someone can be bound. I assume that palapa of Par. has arisen through metanalysis from palapan, and means ``pleasures, relaxation'', enjoyed after one has completed one's work, which took all one's time and energy (``to rest on one's laurels'').

definition: (Skt) five. In a number of compounds it is clearly ``five'' (e.g. pañcamahābhūta, pañcendriya), but elsewhere it seems to designate a collection or completeness, regardless of the exact number, and it is often doubtful whether it is to be regarded as Skt pañca, esp. when it forms a compound with a non-Skt word. This applies also to mañca, para mañca, saṅ amañca (saṅ pañca?).

definition: Prob.: putting together, joining up, applying, fixing up; that which joins up, which meets st.; that which is lying or put ready; that with which one meets or receives so. or st. It seems that this basic meaning can be found in the various ways pasaṅ is used: trap; yoke; stake or piece in a game; incoming tide; time of approaching twilight (pasaṅ sande) and of preparing the lamps (pasaṅ damar); attitude towards or appearance to so. (pasaṅ kuhira, lĕyĕp). 1. trap; yoke; arrangement, making or being ready; appearance, attitude 2. incoming tide, high tide, at high tide 3. stakes in a game; game of chance; perhaps a part. game (kind of chess or draughts) and the pieces which are advanced?

definition: particle denoting continuation, repetition or variation of action or condition (again and again, here and there, in various ways, constantly); in the case of action, usually with the connotation that it is lacking purpose, order or a definite object (at random, haphazardly, aimlessly, unthinkingly, indiscriminately). Pati is often followed by the verb in the form of the base-word, either single (pati purug) or duplicated (pati gagap-gagap), with or without suffix-i (pati tuti, pati gagap-gagapi); see under the relevant base#words. The base-word after patiṅ (rarely pati) receives infixed ĕr or ĕl (patiṅ kĕrĕḍap, patiṅ cĕlĕguk). With negation (tan pati) often: not constantly, not very.

definition: ^4 (patya, paṭi, paṭhi ? ) Orthography uncertain, as evidenced by the MSS (see variants on AW 4.4). Equally uncertain is the etymology. Connection with pāṭhi (see pathya 3.)? with pati III very, exceedingly? with pa + ati? In the different forms which follow the meaning seems to be: to give special attention to, bestow great care on, take great pains over, make a special effort, exert oneself; urgently, passionately.

definition: (Skt ``belonging to the way'', suitable, fit, proper, wholesome, salutary). See also su-. 1. wholesome, salutary (food and drink); appetizing, tasty, savoury, delicious 2. ``following the right (the proper) way'', proper, suitable 3. versed in, expert, competent, clever, able. We can think of a semantic development from 2. Relationship with Skt pāṭhin (one who has read or studied any subject, knowing, conversant with) is also a possibility, however.

(Swarajambu): ikaṅ strī amĕdĕl rare jro wĕtĕṅ, pāpanya pinerakĕnya riṅ saṅ maweh sĕkul iriya; ... ikaṅ śiṣya mwaṅ guru tan pawarah riṅ daśasīla ... pinerakĕnya sapāpanya ri saṅ guru; saṅ prabhu yan tan padaṇḍa maliṅ, sapāpa niṅ maliṅ pinerakĕnya ri saṅ prabhu. My translation differs from that of Jb (``wipe off'') and from the bal. com. which have sadsadin for the RY passages and render OJ usap with perperan; cf ModJ peper, ``a piece of cloth for wiping''. Esp. on account of per, maper, pinerakĕn the meaning given seems preferable. Perhaps AbhW has understood the Wir passages in the other sense.
33.12 to be read: dak peri luh ta, or: dan peri luhta?
(IV2) connects per with pe.

definition: (phīra) (see below) In the curse included in inscriptions (jav. and bal.):

citations.text: 48 (943) vo 35: upadrawa ri dewata dṛṣṭi liputan i phīra muliha rikaṅ naraka ... ;
8 f. (s.a.) end, b.3;
96 f. (1022) VIb.5: saputĕn de niṅ pira sajiwa#kāla, atĕhĕra kapatala (read kapataka?);
14 f. (1071) VI b.1: saṅhapĕn iṅ wuhaya, bhraṣṭa lipatĕn iṅ phira, muliha riṅ narakaloka. But also, in the same context, dhīra: OJO 30 (915) side I, 21: bhraṣṭa liputan i dhīra muliha iṅ kanaraka;

71 (1181) vo 11: saṅhapĕn iṅ wuhaya awuka tan tĕmwasana liputĕn iṅ dhīra wulaṅun ... Goris (index) says: ``phira (Skt., but not in PW) snake''. Based on what information? KBW s.v.) phira: ``to be read dhīra in the sense of ocean?'' MW gives under dhīra: the ocean, sea (as an image of constancy?), but gives no reference. The use of liputĕn and saputĕn makes the interpretation of Goris very improbable. The fact that phira occurs six times and is such a rare word makes it unlikely that phira in the texts stands for dhīra. In similar curse#formulas we find liputĕn connected with agni (liputĕn gĕsĕṅana de saṅ hyaṅ agni): VG VII 32 f. (860) XI.a;
7 (931) VIb.6; Biluluk I (1366) V.4; with kleśa (bhraṣṭā sakula sambandha liputĕn iṅ kleśa): Gor 105 f. (1040) 3;

96 f. (1022) VIIa.2; with maṅdryanya (?) (tumĕmua bhraṣṭa, liputĕn de niṅ maṅdryanya): OJO 42 (929) 7b. Because of the use of liputĕn one expects something of a general nature such as kleśa, wulaṅun. Could it be from pīḍā, ``harm, injury''?

definition: (Skt father, plur. pitaras) forefathers, esp. the deceased ancestors, either of any particular person or the progenitors of mankind generally. In honour of both these classes rites called śrāddha are performed; they inhabit a particular region (either bhuwah or the orbit of the moon) and are considered as the regents of the nakṣatras Maghā and Mūla. See also pitara.

Gonda's derivation (BKI 88 p. 502-507; SI p. 324) from kawaśa (> kośa > prakośa) has the disadvantages that kośa in this meaning is very rare (see kośa II), and that prakosa occurs as early as AWj. definition: the basic meaning seems to be: standing out high above the others, lofty. So in the literal sense of ``elevated'' for instance in Swd. 18.8: (under apuji-puji). Usually, however, in several forms (puji, pujyan, puji-puji, pupuji, apuji, apuji-puji) it is ``lofty'' in the pejorative sense of: feeling oneself superior, arrogant, haughty, giving oneself airs, with much show, aloof, unapproachable; prob. also: feeling elated (``on top of the world''), proud. Amuji (to extol, praise), which is frequent in all texts, seems to belong here too.

In some kidungs Punta is the name of one of Pañji's companions, but it is not clear whether in GK 10.7: punta is a proper name or belongs to Praśānta, and impossible to show that he already possesses the character of the ModJ panakawan. See also ḍapunta.
